mission.title: Install and Configure Containerd on Kubernetes
mission.description: containerd is an industry-standard container runtime that manages the complete lifecycle of containers on a host system. Installing containerd allows you to run and manage containers efficiently in your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the containerd Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official containerd Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add containerd https://charts.containerd.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install containerd using Helm
steps.description:
Install containerd in your Kubernetes cluster using the following command:
```bash
helm install containerd containerd/containerd --version v2.2.1 --namespace containerd --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the containerd pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace containerd
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for containerd
steps.description:
Set resource limits for the containerd deployment to ensure it runs efficiently:
```bash
kubectl patch deployment containerd -n containerd --type='json' -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources", "value": {"limits": {"cpu": "500m", "memory": "512Mi"}, "requests": {"cpu": "250m", "memory": "256Mi"}}}]'
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for containerd
steps.description:
Forward a local port to the containerd service to access it:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/containerd -n containerd 8000:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of containerd will show the containerd pods running in the 'containerd' namespace. You can access the service through port forwarding and ensure that resource limits are applied correctly.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add containerd https://charts.containerd.io
helm repo update
helm install containerd containerd/containerd --version v2.2.1 --namespace containerd --create-namespace

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the containerd Helm release from your Kubernetes cluster using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall containerd --namespace containerd
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with containerd:
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace containerd
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep containerd | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the containerd namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace containerd
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of the containerd installation:
```bash
helm get values containerd --namespace containerd > containerd-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the containerd release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade containerd containerd/containerd --version v2.3.0 --namespace containerd
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the containerd pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace containerd
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the containerd pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace containerd
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be wrong with the configuration or resources.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the containerd service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/containerd -n containerd 8000:80
```
Also, verify that the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace containerd
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to insufficient resources, check the resource limits and requests set for the containerd deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment containerd -n containerd
```
Adjust the resource limits if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade command fails, check the error message for details. You can roll back to the previous version using:
```bash
helm rollback containerd <revision-number> --namespace containerd
```
List the revisions with:
```bash
helm history containerd --namespace containerd
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
